Founding of the Khalsa

# Founding of the Khalsa

### And the 5 Ks

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Apr 14, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

After Guru Teg Bahadur was tortured to death and executed by Aurangzeb in November, 1675, his son [Guru Gobind Singh](https://www.quora.com/Sikhism-What-is-the-significance-of-5-ks-kesh-kangha-kachcha-kripan-and-kada-for-a-Sikh/answer/Ratnakar-Sadasyula-%E0%B0%B0%E0%B0%A4%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%A8%E0%B0%BE%E0%B0%95%E0%B0%B0%E0%B1%8D-%E0%B0%B8%E0%B0%A6%E0%B0%B8%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%AF%E0%B1%81%E0%B0%B2), felt the need for the Sikhs to transform themselves into a warrior force, both to defend the Sikh faith and attack the Mughal Army. He sent hukumnamas( letters of authority) to all his followers, asking them to congregate at Anandpur Sahib, on Baisakhi in 1699.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!3KxF!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F046d5d30-3229-4c56-b4cc-cb6b408fc372_602x401.webp)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!3KxF!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F046d5d30-3229-4c56-b4cc-cb6b408fc372_602x401.webp)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**March 30,1699-Anandpur Sahib, Baisakhi.**

On a small hill called Keshgharh, at Anandpur Sahib, Guru Gobind Singh, addressed his followers and said that today the Guru demands from his followers. His followers replied "*Hukum Karo, Sache Patshah*" meaning Order us, True Lord.

Guru Gobind Singh drew his sword, and asked for a volunteer, who would willingly sacrifice his head. On the 3rd call, a man called Daya Ram, willingly stepped forward, the Guru, took him inside the tent, and later emerged, blood dripping from the sword. And then 4 more volunteers came forward offering to do the same. The Guru came out with the 5 volunteers unharmed from the tent who were called the***Panj Piare( the 5 beloved).***They were**Daya Ram( Bhai Daya Singh), Dharam Das( Bhai Dharam Singh), Himmat Rai( Bhai Himmat Singh), Mokham Chand(Bhai Mokham Singh) and Sahib Chand( Bhai Sahib Singh).**

These 5 men were administered the***Amrit***, basically a mixture of water with sugar crystals, that was stirred by the Guru with a sword. This act symbolized the founding of the Khalsa, the sword representing the will and strength to fight oppression, while the sweetened water represented that the actions shall be only for protection but not out of hatred. The 5 men were made to repeat the phrase*"Waheguru ka Khalsa, Waheguru ki Phateh"*( The Khalsa belongs to the Supreme Teacher, the Victory belongs to the Supreme Teacher). The men were christened as Singh( meaning Lion) while the women were christened as Kaur( Princess).

**The 5 K's**are basically the 5 articles of faith, that the Khalsa Sikhs must wear at all times, especially those who have undergone the baptization. They form the external identity and the commitment to the Sikh way of life. There are two kinds here, those who have been baptized by Amrit, and use all 5 K's are known as*Amritdhari*Sikhs, while those who have not taken the Amrit, but use the 5 K's are known as*Sahajdhari.*

**Kesh( Hair)**- The practice of letting the hair grow naturally, without shaving or cutting it. Basically hair growing naturally was considered will of God, and it was forbidden to use a razor for shaving or cutting. Of late though, many have taken to cutting the hair,though they follow the other tenets of Sikhism. While some do it for convenience, others in the West, have been doing so, to avoid racial attacks, especially those that happened post 9/11, when they were mistaken for Muslims.

**Kanga(comb)-**Guru Gobind Singh mandated the use of Kanga or comb, basically to comb the hair, and also keep it neat, tidy and knotted. The Kanga was meant to symbolize the importance of cleanliness and discipline in the life of a Sikh. Sikhs who wear the turban and do not cut their hair, use the Kanga compulsorily.

**Kara( Bracelet)-**This is a steel bracelet worn by both male and female Sikhs initiated into the order. Though it is worn even by Punjabi Hindus and Muslims too. This symbolizes the attachment to God, as represented by it's round shape, that means no beginning or end, much like the eternal nature of God. It is also a reminder to follow the path of God, and carry out the dharma as prescribed by Guru.

**Kachera or Kaccha(Undergarments)-**One of the 5 K's mandated by Guru Gobind Singh worn by both male and female Sikhs. It covers the private parts of the body, and also a reminder to control one's lust( Kaam). Practically it was much more easier to maintain and wash it, compared to other undergarments. Also considering the Sikhs regularly had to fight battles, the Kaccha, was convenient for them while engaging in combat.

**Kirpan( Dagger)-**The ceremonial dagger worn by all baptized Sikhs, derives from two words Kripa( mercy) and Aan( Honor,dignity). Symbolically speaking, it represents the power of truth to cut through falsehood. The Kirpan can be used to prevent violence against a defenseless and weak person. As the Guru himself explained*'When all means to keep peace fail, it's righteous to rise the sword'.*Of late though carrying of Kirpan has become a contentious issue in some Western nations where Sikhs have settled. Denmark has made it illegal for Sikhs to carry a kirpan,while Belgium, UK,Canada allow it as a religious symbol. In the US, there have been court cases on carrying a kirpan, though New York and Ohio, have ruled that Sikhs could carry it, as long as it was not used.
